schools.summary: The chapter is a seam joining the two books of Samuel; Ellicott and Clarke stitch it to 1 Samuel 30-31, and Keil surveys the whole reign David's kingdom will unfold.
Matthew Henry reads Ziklag for its providence: mighty men flock to David, yet he posts no spies for Saul's death — 'he that believes does not make haste.'
Gill fixes the moment as the third day after the battle.
Wesley notes Ziklag, though burned, was not so consumed that David's men could not lodge there.
Ellicott reads the obeisance as recognition of David's new kingship, not mere respect; Keil follows the running dialogue at face value.
Clarke calls the whole account a self-contradictory fabrication; the only fact is the crown and bracelets, plundered from the field.
Gill counts the third day from the battle in which Saul fell.

sides.position: a fabrication invented to win reward; only the plundered regalia is fact
the general facts (defeat, deaths) are true, whatever the particulars

schools.summary: Ellicott argues the man really was in Israel's camp; the general facts of defeat and death are true, though later particulars are doubtful.
Gill notes David already suspects the man from his appearance and circumstances.

debates.question: Was the Amalekite actually in the army of Israel?

sides.position: yes — he claims it, and no bystander strays into a battle; 'by chance' refers to finding Saul
his presence was merely accidental (from the 'by chance' of v.6)

schools.summary: Gill and Keil trace the rising catalogue of loss — rout, slaughter, and the death of Saul and Jonathan — in the messenger's answer.
Henry: the man names only Saul and Jonathan, the one David most feared and the one he most loved, and expects to be rewarded as a bringer of good tidings.
Keil makes the question the hinge opening the man's circumstantial narrative of vv.6-10.
Henry: David is slow to believe and will not press his own claim to the throne until fully assured.
Gill notes David addressed the young man who brought the tidings.
Ellicott reconstructs the flight up Gilboa and tests the tale against 1 Samuel 31, judging a solitary Saul unlikely; Keil grants the regalia put the substance beyond doubt.
Gill rejects the rabbinic guess that the messenger was Doeg or Doeg's son.
Gill: Saul looked behind to see how near the pursuing enemy had come.
The vivid, circumstantial staging is read as the Amalekite's calculated self-insertion into Saul's last moments, at odds with 1 Samuel 31.

themes: David lets the witness commit himself

schools.summary: Ellicott rehearses the hereditary Amalekite feud with Israel and supposes the man attached to, or conscripted into, Saul's army.
Clarke (with Delaney) marks the irony that an Amalekite strips the crown Saul forfeited by sparing Amalek.
Gill: Saul asked in order to know whether the man behind him was friend or enemy.
Ellicott: the rare word for 'anguish' is uncertain — cramp, giddiness, or some effect of the wound disabling Saul.
Gill doubts Saul would beg death of an Amalekite, having shunned death by the Philistines.
Henry: a king weary of life and dignity is a fearful emblem — unpardoned guilt makes death the king of terrors.

debates.question: Did Saul really speak this plea to the Amalekite?

sides.position: doubtful — the request rings false and suits the man's invention
taken as spoken, it reveals the misery of a guilty death

schools.summary: Ellicott judges the killing a lie, inconsistent with 1 Samuel 31, yet the crown and bracelet prove the man reached the body and recognised David as king.
Henry: Saul's pride wore the regalia into battle; providence brings the long-awaited crown to David by an ill-designing man.
Clarke examines the diadem and bracelet as ensigns of royalty, given when investing dominion.
Gill supplies the grim mechanics of pressing the spear home.

debates.question: Did the Amalekite really kill Saul?

sides.position: no — a lie for reward, contradicting 1 Samuel 31; only the regalia is genuine
the report is taken at face value as far as the deed

schools.summary: Henry: David rends his clothes and mourns even for Saul his enemy; his psalms against foes sprang from holy zeal, not revenge — he would not gloat over the fallen.
Gill clarifies that David laid hold on his own clothes, not the messenger's.

themes: not rejoicing at a foe's fall

schools.summary: Ellicott separates the fourfold mourning — for Saul, Jonathan, the people of the LORD, and the house of Israel — and rejects the LXX's 'people of Judah'; Keil notes the man's alien descent.
Gill vouches for the sincerity of the weeping and fasting.
Keil: the man is a resident 'son of a stranger,' an Amalekite emigrant living under Israel's law — which removes any excuse of ignorance.
Gill records Abarbanel's alternative, that David asked to whom the man belonged as a servant.

Ellicott and Keil: the man is judged by his own account; David's earlier sparing of 'the Lord's anointed' (1 Samuel 24; 26) measures his horror, and the mouth convicts whether the boast be true or false.
Gill: David understood the boast to mean the man drew his sword and slew the king.

themes: judged out of his own mouth

schools.summary: Ellicott vindicates the execution: the man had hailed David as king and confessed to high treason; David's band lived by the sword.
Gill: David summoned one of his own attendants to carry out the sentence.
Clarke: whether or not the boast was true, the man was justly executed on his own confession.
Gill reads the blood-guilt formula: he who shed blood must have his blood shed, proved by his own confession.
Keil's maxim: the mouth testifies against its owner, condemning him done or not done.
Ellicott names the genre — a funeral dirge like David's over Abner and Jeremiah's over Josiah — and calls it David's finest secular poetry.
Henry: casting grief into verse makes it more moving and lasting; David generously conceals Saul's faults and praises only what was true.
Gill: David composed and sang the elegy in a fitting tune.
Clarke reads 'the bow' as the song's title (Kesheth), like the Psalm-titles; Keil lays out the ode's three strophes each opening 'How are the mighty fallen.'
Gill takes 'the use of the bow' as a real military measure — training Judah against the Philistine archers.
Henry gives both readings — the weapon of war or the title of the song — and notes the elegy is preserved in the book of Jasher.

debates.question: Does 'the bow' mean the weapon or the title of the song?

sides.position: the title of the elegy (Kesheth), named for Jonathan's bow
the weapon — David trains Judah in archery against the Philistines

schools.summary: Ellicott takes 'beauty' as the glory of Israel (Saul and Jonathan) and rejects the Syriac 'gazelle'; the line is the song's superscription and its refrain the structural pillar.
Gill: the flower of the nation — king, prince, and young men — lies slain on Gilboa.
Wesley unfolds 'how' into 'how strangely, how suddenly, how universally.'

debates.question: What does 'the beauty of Israel' mean?

sides.position: the glory or ornament of Israel — Saul and Jonathan
'the gazelle,' a poetic name for Jonathan (Syriac)

themes: the refrain 'how are the mighty fallen'

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil map Gath and Askelon, the proverb 'tell it not in Gath' (Micah 1:10), and the singing women who celebrate victories (Exodus 15; 1 Samuel 18).
Gill: not a command David could enforce, but a wish it were not so.
Wesley: not a precept but a poetical wish expressing sorrow at the dishonour to God and his people.

themes: a poetic wish, not a command

schools.summary: Keil reads the curse as summoning nature to mourn; Ellicott takes 'anointed' of the oiled shield, not Saul; Clarke emends 'not' to 'weapons.'
Henry: a poetical malediction like Job's cursing of his day — the earth's fruit depends on heaven and is devoted to heaven (Joel 1:9).
Wesley: no proper imprecation but passionate horror; the cast-away shield is the shame of Saul dying like a common soldier.

debates.question: To what does 'as though he had not been anointed with oil' refer?

sides.position: the shield, which was oiled for preservation and thrown away uncared for
Saul — as though he had not been a king (Vulgate); Clarke emends to 'weapons anointed with oil'

outliers.note: Emends beli ('not') to keley ('weapons') and prints the whole ode in Hebrew and Kennicott's Latin, rendering 'slain' as 'soldier.'

schools.summary: Keil unfolds the imagery of arrows drinking blood and the sword devouring flesh (Deuteronomy 32; Isaiah 34; Jeremiah 46), dividing bow and sword between Jonathan and Saul.
Wesley: their weapons never missed; past success is named as an aggravation of their final overthrow.
Gill: Jonathan's bow always did execution and never returned in vain.
Keil and Ellicott: 'lovely and pleasant' applies chiefly to Jonathan but truly to Saul in his early years; David recalls the amiable and forgets the injury (eagle, lion; Habakkuk 1:8).
Wesley excuses Saul's fierceness as sudden passion and jealousy of state, not his ordinary temper.
Gill stresses their concord in court and camp, divided only over David.
Ellicott reads the scarlet and gold as the fruit of Saul's victorious campaigns — real prosperity brought to Israel despite his fall.
Wesley: the daughters are addressed as makers of songs and lovers of ornament; Saul's spoils and peace enriched them.
Gill sets the mourning women within the scene of the elegy.
Keil: the refrain repeats v.19 to open the second strophe, which narrows to Jonathan alone.
Wesley: 'thine high places' are the hills of Jonathan's lost inheritance, forfeited by his father's sin.
Gill: the repeated refrain measures how deeply the loss affected David.
Keil and Ellicott: 'passing the love of women' likens Jonathan's love to a faithful wife's — a devotion possible only between noble souls united in the fear of God.
Henry: the wonder is that Jonathan loved the man who would take his crown; a true friend is life's chief delight and his loss its chief distress.
Gill: David mourned not Jonathan's happy eternal state but the loss of his counsel and friendship.
Keil: a brief aftertone closing the ode; 'the weapons of war' is a figure for the heroes themselves (Isaiah 13:5; so Luther, 'die Streitbaren').
Henry: 'the mighty have fallen' is thrice lamented; public losses are laid to heart by men of public spirit, and David grieves though he hopes to repair them.

themes: the dying fall of the ode
